hints: E[Y|X=x]=e^{x/2} (lognormal mean). Use Cov=E[XY]ŌłÆE[X]E[Y] with the tower rule.
E[Y]=E[e^{X/2}]=2; E[XY]=E[X e^{X/2}]=4 (Exp(1) integrals).
Cov=4ŌłÆ1┬Ę2=2.
solution: Since $Y\mid X=x\sim\mathrm{LogNorm}(0,x)$, $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]=e^{X/2}$. With $X\sim\mathrm{Exp}(1)$: $\mathbb{E}[Y]=\mathbb{E}[e^{X/2}]=\int_0^\infty e^{x/2}e^{-x}dx=2$ and $\mathbb{E}[XY]=\mathbb{E}[Xe^{X/2}]=\int_0^\infty x\,e^{-x/2}dx=\tfrac{1}{(1/2)^2}=4$. Thus $\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y)=4-1\cdot2=2.$

hints: Among strings with no 'HH', use the transfer matrix [[1,1],[1,0]] (states T,H).
The limiting frequency of H equals u_H v_H/(u┬Ęv) with the Perron eigenvectors: 1/(╬╗┬▓+1).
╬╗┬▓+1=(5+ŌłÜ5)/2 ŌćÆ density 2/(5+ŌłÜ5): a=2,b=5,c=5 ŌćÆ abc=50.
solution: Strings with no consecutive heads form the golden-mean shift with transfer matrix $M=\begin{psmallmatrix}1&1\\1&0\end{psmallmatrix}$ (states $T,H$) and Perron eigenvalue $\lambda=\tfrac{1+\sqrt5}2$. Both eigenvectors are $(\lambda,1)$, so the limiting fraction of $H$ positions is $\dfrac{u_H v_H}{u\cdot v}=\dfrac{1}{\lambda^2+1}$. Since $\lambda^2+1=\tfrac{5+\sqrt5}2$, the limit is $\dfrac{2}{5+\sqrt5}$. Hence $a=2,b=5,c=5$ and $abc=50.$

hints: Stopping the first time heads exceed tails relates to Dyck-path/ballot counts.
Summing the resulting ratio over stopping times uses the arcsin Taylor series at x=1.
The expected ratio works out to ŽĆ/4 Ōēł 0.785.
solution: Stopping the first time heads exceed tails, the stopping-length probabilities are Catalan/Dyck-type terms. Summing the head-ratio against these weights collapses (via the given $\arcsin$ series evaluated toward $x=1$) to $\mathbb{E}[a]=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\approx0.785.$

hints: Number of aces in 13 cards is hypergeometric.
solution:
The number of aces among $13$ drawn is hypergeometric:
$$P=\frac{\binom{4}{2}\binom{48}{11}}{\binom{52}{13}}=6\cdot\frac{(13\cdot12)(39\cdot38)}{52\cdot51\cdot50\cdot49}\approx0.21.$$

hints: He was x in year x┬▓, so born in x┬▓ŌłÆx, between 1751 and 1871.
Need x┬▓Ōēż1871 ŌćÆ xŌēż43; check x=43.
43┬▓=1849, born 1806 ŌĆö valid ŌćÆ x=43.
solution: Being $x$ years old in year $x^2$ means he was born in $x^2-x$, which must lie between $1751$ and $1871$. Since $x^2\le1871$, $x\le43$; $x=43$ gives $43^2=1849$ and birth year $1806$, consistent. So $x=43.$

hints: Kevin holds a call on X with strike 0.5; fair price 0.70 = E[max(XŌłÆ0.5,0)].
For X~Exp(scale ╬▓), E[max(XŌłÆK,0)] = ╬▓ e^{ŌłÆK/╬▓}.
Solve 0.70 = ╬▓ e^{ŌłÆ0.5/╬▓} ŌćÆ ╬▓ Ōēł 1.1.
solution: Kevin's contract pays $\max(X-0.5,0)$ (a call struck at $0.5$). For $X\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\text{scale }\beta)$, $\mathbb{E}[\max(X-K,0)]=\beta e^{-K/\beta}$. Fair pricing requires $0.70=\beta e^{-0.5/\beta}$; solving gives $\beta\approx1.1.$

hints: A uniform surface point: probabilities are area ratios; use 4ŽĆr┬▓ and Archimedes' zone law.
Bottom sphere (r=14, 0ŌĆō28 ft) all Ōēż39; middle (r=11, center at 39) contributes its lower half; top all >39.
solution:
The bottom sphere ($r=14$) spans heights $0$ŌĆō$28$ (all $\le39$): area $4\pi\cdot196$. The middle sphere ($r=11$) is centered at $39$, so exactly its lower half is $\le39$: area $\tfrac12\cdot4\pi\cdot121=2\pi\cdot121$. The top sphere lies above $50$: nothing. Total area $=4\pi(196+121+64)$. So
$$P=\frac{4\pi\cdot196+2\pi\cdot121}{4\pi(381)}=\frac{1026}{1524}=\frac{171}{254}.$$

hints: Disjoint means no element is in both A and B.
Per element, 4 equally likely (in A, in B, both, neither); not-both has prob 3/4.
P(disjoint)=(3/4)^5; P(not disjoint)=1ŌłÆ243/1024=781/1024.
solution: For each of the $5$ elements, its membership in $(A,B)$ is one of $4$ equally likely cases; disjointness needs it not to be in both, probability $\tfrac34$. By independence $P(\text{disjoint})=(\tfrac34)^5=\tfrac{243}{1024}$, so $P(\text{not disjoint})=1-\tfrac{243}{1024}=\tfrac{781}{1024}.$

hints: Same setup as perfect-correlation-I but now Žā_X>Žā_Y.
Žü(X+Y,XŌłÆY)=(Žā_X┬▓ŌłÆŽā_Y┬▓)/[(Žā_X+Žā_Y)|Žā_XŌłÆŽā_Y|].
With Žā_X>Žā_Y the sign is +1.
╬Ż ╬ö┬▓ has mean t, so the expression is Var(╬Ż ╬ö┬▓).
Each ╬ö ~ N(0, ╬┤) with ╬┤=t2^{ŌłÆn}; Var(╬ö┬▓)=2╬┤┬▓. Sum over 2^n terms.
solution: $\sum_m\Delta_{m,n}^2$ has mean $t$, so the quantity is $\mathrm{Var}\big(\sum_m\Delta_{m,n}^2\big)$. Each increment is $N(0,\delta)$ with $\delta=t2^{-n}$, and $\mathrm{Var}(\Delta^2)=2\delta^2$. Summing the $2^n$ independent terms: $2^n\cdot2(t2^{-n})^2=2t^2 2^{-n}$. At $t=1,n=5$: $2\cdot2^{-5}=\tfrac1{16}.$

hints: 'Only odd elements' restricts to {1,3,5,7,9}; the only odd members of {1,2,3} are 1,3.
Exactly 2 from {1,2,3} forces including both 1 and 3.
Free choices: any subset of {5,7,9} ŌćÆ 2┬│=8.
solution: Only odd elements are allowed, so the subset lies in $\{1,3,5,7,9\}$. The odd elements of $\{1,2,3\}$ are just $1,3$, so 'exactly two from $\{1,2,3\}$' forces both $1$ and $3$ in. The remaining elements are any subset of $\{5,7,9\}$: $2^3=8.$

hints: P(DŌēżr)=r┬▓/100 (area ratio). Write your expected winnings.
W(r)=ŌłÆ(10ŌłÆr)(r┬▓/100)+r(1ŌłÆr┬▓/100) simplifies to r ŌłÆ r┬▓/10.
Maximize: W'(r)=1ŌłÆr/5=0 ŌćÆ r=5.
solution:
Since the point is uniform on the disk, $P(D\le r)=\tfrac{r^2}{100}$. Your expected winnings are
$$W(r)=-(10-r)\tfrac{r^2}{100}+r\Big(1-\tfrac{r^2}{100}\Big)=r-\tfrac{r^2}{10}.$$
Then $W'(r)=1-\tfrac r5=0\Rightarrow r=5.$
